Gransden's Penguin collection ranges from the earliest translators to
twentieth century exponents, with a number of pieces illustrating
Vergil's influence on poets through the ages. Twentieth century poets
who have imitated or responded to Vergil are: V. Sackville-West, Allen
Tate, Robert Lowell, Oliver St John Gogarty, and W. H. Auden. Lacaj
provides the first translation of the epic into Albanian.

H.C. Schneider, "Tiere, Tod, und Jenseits Vorstellungen am
Beispiel des Culex der Appendix Vergiliana," Hans-Joachim Drexhage,
Julie Sünskes Thompson, Tod, Bestattung und Jenseits in der
griechisch-römischen Antike (St. Katharinen 1994) 134-147.
Gorman examines borrowings from Vergil in the С iris from a thematic
perspective, explicating the factors that led the Ciris poet to imitate
particular passages and the use to which he put these imitations to
enhance the poem. Gorman argues that the poet's allusion to the Nisus
and Eury alus episode ( Aeneid 9,168-449) reveals verbal and thematic
manipulation of that celebrated episode to enhance his own story and
flaunt his learning.

raw_text:
Vergil recomposes the melody in a major key. Hubbard interprets
Eclogues 1-3 as the first act in an intertextual drama of Vergil's
tentative poetic self-emergence; Eclogues 4-6 are more broadly
intergeneric contextual confrontations that culminate in the
juxtaposition of Vergil (Menalcas) and Gallus in Eclogues 9 and 10,
both masters of their separate but inter-related generic spheres. Kowara
argues that Vergil used elision as a subtle method to differentiate the
singers; Menalcas claims the lion's share of technical polish. Perkell
senses the power of pastoral to effect response and to change values
(vis-a-vis Tityrus), but Vergil leaves the conclusion open, the future
actions of both characters unrevealed. "This lack of clarity, this real
impossibility of an uncomplicated and definitive moral, is Vergil's
hallmark" (81). Perkell's sustained reading of Eclogue 10 accents
Vergil's allusion to Theocritus, Idyll 1 . The allusion is ironic: Gallus
differs markedly from the Daphnis model.

raw_text:
Christine Perkell, The Poet's Truth: A Study of the Poet in
Virgil's Georgics. Berkeley, Los Angeles, 1989. Rev: Davis, Scholia 1
(1992) 119-124; Horsfall, CR 45 (1995) 442-443.
Dalzell's lectures provide a close analysis of Lucretius' s De Rerum
Natura, Vergil's Georgics, and Ovid's Ars Amatoria. His aim is to
refocus critical attention on didactic poetry, to consider its purpose,
history and character. Gale treats the complex, shifting world view of
the Georgics, man's relationship with the world, gods, and other men. In
so doing, Vergil offers a commentary on the traditional views and on
the conflicting values of the poets who shaped the polyphonic tradition.
The Georgics are concerned with rerum natura, man's place in the
world, and with the didactic tradition itself.

Duke University, 1994.
Franchi deals with aetiology and ideology in Vergil's epic. Gaskin's
concern with the epic's "losers" reaches its tentative resolution at the
close of his Eirene article: "The Aeneid ends, in a typically Virgilian
fashion, in neither triumph nor despair, although more in despair than
in triumph; but not really that either, more in what one might, at the risk
of solecism, call a passionate melancholy." (96) James is occupied with
the ambiguity of condere, "to found and to bury the sword" at the end
thereby highlighting the cultural progenitors in a form of civil conflict.
Spence recalls the symposium on 11 March, 1995 at the University of
Georgia entitled "After Grief and Reason: Poets and Critics Read
Vergil." The proceedings of the conference and didactic tapes are
available on videotape for use in classrooms through Prof. Sarah
Spence, Department of Classics, University of Georgia, Athens GA
30602 USA.

favor the Dictator over Augustus in Aeneid 1, 286-296, and argues
forcefully for that identification. Dyer takes proruptum (246) as an
accusative supine after mare as its direct object meaning "to cause to
burst forth," and "yielding." His translation: "(Timavus) goes to make
the sea surge forward and oppresses the farmed fields with the roaring
salt sea." Petter asks for a naturalistic rather than an impressionistic
reading of the Laocoon episode; he concludes that only the sons die

examines the boat race in Aeneid 5 as reflection of the Homeric model
but recast as a contemporary circus spectacle, an image of cosmic and
political order, restaging the past in a familiar and popular form of
Roman civic life. Casali wonders what additional Daedalic panels
Aeneas could have lingered over, perhaps scenes from the life of
Daedalus, or from the careers of Theseus and Ariadne. Kilpatrick is
concerned with the actual properties of horn and ivory as materials used
in ancient architecture and technology, and with the natural optics of
horn (transparent) and ivory (opaque): "the gate of ivory is a
confirmation ...that the katabasis is a dream-vision, not literal narrative
reality." (67) Molyviati-Toptsi argues that falsa insomnia is an authorial
comment on the deceptive quality of Anchises' speech within the
dramatic context; the speech was an invention to encourage Aeneas to
fulfil his mission. Weber predicates allegoresis as a valid approach to
the Aeneid based on Vergil's familiarity with Hellenistic allegorizations
of Homer. Vergil not only "confronts his hero with the human passions
that Lucretius saw allegorized in Tartarus", he also "adopts aspects of
the Lucretian theory of the soul out of which he created his own
allegory of the tree and its magical bough." (33) Lucretian imagery
permeates the Vergilian allegory. Penwill highlights Vergil's allusions
to philosophic myth in Aeneid 6, particularly to Plato's "Myth of Er"
and to Cicero's Somnium Scipionis. The hero's departure with the Sibyl
through the ivory gate implies, for Penwill, that "anyone who uses the
dead as a source of moral instruction for the living, who looks to the
past to find a moral paradigm for the present, who seeks ...an ethical
imperative from the other side, is necessarily and inevitably deluding
both himself and his fellow human beings." (27)

raw_text:
J. Swanepoel, "Infelix Dido: Vergil and the Notion of the
Tragic," Akroterion 40 (1995) 30-46.
Briquel combines an Etruscan 7th century vase with the legend Laucies
Mezenties (Lucius Mezentius), the "Aeneas" tumulus at Lavinium, and
ancient testimony in his analysis of Vergil's construction of the
contemptor deum. Hollman refers to the Ugaritic legend of the model
son, Aqat, and the Etruscan Aeneas statuettes at Veii in his argument for
a Punic "Aeneas". Reckford measures Venus' help, advice and
encouragement, and Aeneas' frustration, against Homeric paradigms
and the Hymn to Aphrodite, and explores possible implications of
Venus as seductive mother for Aeneas' s psychology, for Dido's
tragedy, and for the Iliadic half of the epic.

Braccesi (145-191) examines the tradition of Idomeneus and the
Locrian survivor of Troy in the Salento ( Aeneid 3,399-401). Clark
examines letters of Giles of Viterbo, humanist and cardinal, recording
his Grand Tour of the Phlegraean Fields; his response to the Avernian
region is colored by post-Vergilian legends and Christian associations.
Zevi rehearses the impact of Euboeans on the Bay of Naples and
examines the implications of the Daedalic landing at Cumae
highlighted by Vergil.

Rosemary Zurawel, "Exploring the Moral Dilemma in
Selections from the A ene id and Metamorphoses, with Applications in
Character Education," NECJ 23 (1996) 141-146.
Galinsky re-examines the final scene with attention to associations and
relevant Homeric scenes. He proposes, evaluates, and rejects a number
of possible alternative responses and adduces Philodemus's De Ira and
the Epicurean view of anger to shed new light on the final episode.
Vergil's audience had expectations beyond simple reliance on
Peripatetic and Stoic philosophy. Miller argues that Vergil's vision of
Apollo is indebted, inter alia, to Homer and Augustan ideology. The
dissonance in Vergil's healing Apollo acts as a counterweight to his
appearances elsewhere in the epic, and the composite vision of the god
is "not unrelated to the two sides of Apollo's message to Ascanius
( Aen . 9, 641-644)." (110-111) Zurawel's pedagogical exercise is
concerned with moral questions and problems in Aeneid 2, 4, and 12.

O'Hara's richly annotated, comprehensive collection of examples of
etymological wordplay in Vergil's opera considers the historical context
and analyzes the form, style and significance of the poet's wordplay.
Putnam studies Vergil's description of the tamed stag as something
unique, without parallel in Vergil's primary model, the Homeric epics.
The artwork ekphrasis, the tale of Ganymede woven on the cloak
presented as prize to Cloanthus in the boat race of Aeneid 5, 250-257,
in Putnam's argument, figures as a paradigm for the epic as a whole and
enables readers to reformulate major concerns of the host poem.
Schork examines Aeneid 3, 522-524, 4, 345-347, 408, and 7, 122-123
for key-word ( Italia ) echoes.
